critic_raw:
This experiment compares a 2-layer LSTM versus logistic regression for 30-day readmission prediction on 24 months of single-hospital EHR data.

The RNN input sequence encodes visits using the 'top 50 diagnoses' as one-hot encoded features with a catch-all 'other' category. The vocabulary of 50 codes is fit on training data. In a 24-month training window, the 50 most frequent diagnoses likely reflect the case-mix of the training period. If the hospital's case-mix shifts between months 1–12 (training) and months 13–18 (validation) or 19–24 (test) — which is common due to seasonal variation, protocol changes, or population shifts — some diagnoses that become frequent in the test period will be mapped to the 'other' category. This is silent information loss that would systematically degrade the RNN's ability to use diagnosis information on the most clinically relevant new cases, while the logistic regression baseline uses a pre-specified set of high-risk diagnosis indicators that are clinically defined rather than frequency-defined.

Early stopping with patience=10 on validation loss is used for the LSTM. With ~5,000 validation records (50% of the 10,000 total split across 18-month validation and 6-month test windows... actually the stated split is 12-month training), the actual validation set is roughly months 13–18 which at approximately 5,000 annual admissions represents about 2,500 records. Early stopping on 2,500 records with imbalanced classes (15–20% positive) will be noisy, and the patience=10 parameter means training could stop after a single unlucky batch epoch. The LSTM may be undertrained due to noisy early stopping criteria on a small validation set.

The logistic regression baseline uses 5-fold cross-validation nested within the training set for regularization strength selection, while the LSTM uses hold-out validation. This is not equivalent tuning methodology: nested cross-validation on 12 months of training data is a more statistically stable estimator than single-split hold-out validation for one model, while the other uses hold-out. If the logistic regression has an asymmetrically better regularization estimate, the comparison is unfair in the logistic regression's favor.

The design proposes a non-sequential MLP neural baseline to test whether LSTM gains come from sequencing or model capacity. This is a good control. However, the MLP is described as using 'concatenated visit features,' which means its input dimensionality scales with the maximum sequence length (50 visits × feature dimension). This is a much higher-dimensional input than the LSTM's sequential representation and may introduce regularization challenges that penalize the MLP unfairly.
all_issues_raised: The top-50 ICD-10 vocabulary is frequency-defined from training data. Case-mix shifts between training and test periods will relegate newly frequent diagnoses to the 'other' category, creating silent information loss for the RNN on the most clinically relevant cases in the test period.

Early stopping with patience=10 on approximately 2,500 validation records with 15–20% positive rate is susceptible to noisy stopping decisions, potentially causing the LSTM to be undertrained relative to its capacity.

Logistic regression is tuned via nested 5-fold cross-validation while the LSTM uses single-split hold-out validation. These estimators have different variances, making the comparison methodology asymmetric rather than equivalent.

The proposed MLP non-sequential baseline uses concatenated visit features with input dimensionality proportional to maximum sequence length, creating a high-dimensional input that is penalized differently by regularization than the LSTM's sequential representation.
all_issues_adjudicated: The top-50 ICD-10 vocabulary is frequency-defined from training data. Case-mix shifts between training and test periods will relegate newly frequent diagnoses to the 'other' category, creating silent information loss for the RNN on the most clinically relevant cases in the test period.

Early stopping with patience=10 on approximately 2,500 validation records with 15–20% positive rate is susceptible to noisy stopping decisions, potentially causing the LSTM to be undertrained relative to its capacity.

Logistic regression is tuned via nested 5-fold cross-validation while the LSTM uses single-split hold-out validation. These estimators have different variances, making the comparison methodology asymmetric rather than equivalent.

The proposed MLP non-sequential baseline uses concatenated visit features with input dimensionality proportional to maximum sequence length, creating a high-dimensional input that is penalized differently by regularization than the LSTM's sequential representation.
